questions.question: What is the strongest reason to clarify before drawing architecture in a design interview?
options: Different scope and priorities can change the entire system choice.
Clarification is only useful for junior candidates.
Architecture is always the same regardless of constraints.
Capacity math is impossible after any clarification.
questions.explanation: Scope and priorities determine design direction.

questions.question: Why is rough sizing expected before the deep dive?
options: It anchors decisions about storage, throughput, and bottlenecks.
It makes trade-offs unnecessary.
It guarantees the interviewer agrees with every assumption.
It replaces the need for system diagrams.

questions.question: Which behavior best signals senior judgment during a drill?
options: Choosing one high-value deep dive and defending it clearly.
Listing every component ever used in similar systems.
Avoiding trade-offs to keep the answer short.
Waiting silently for the interviewer to define every assumption.
questions.explanation: Depth selection is a core senior signal.

questions.question: What is the better way to discuss a failure mode in an interview?
options: Name how you detect it and what mitigation changes the outcome.
Say 'add monitoring' and move on immediately.
Avoid failures so the design sounds confident.
Only mention failures after the interviewer insists twice.
questions.explanation: Detection and mitigation make failure analysis concrete.

questions.question: What trade-off is created by spending time on one deep dive?
options: You gain credibility in one critical area but have less time for broad coverage elsewhere.
You eliminate the need for clarification.
You guarantee a perfect final answer.
You no longer need to discuss observability.
questions.explanation: Time spent on depth is time not spent elsewhere.

questions.question: Which signal best shows a redesign answer is real rather than superficial?
options: A specific component, policy, or data path changes because the constraint changed.
The candidate repeats the same architecture more loudly.
The candidate says the system already handles everything.
The candidate adds a cache without explaining why.
questions.explanation: A real redesign has concrete consequences.

questions.question: Why should assumptions be stated even when the prompt is ambiguous?
options: They keep the design moving and give the interviewer a chance to correct important scope choices.
They guarantee all assumptions are correct.
They remove the need for requirements prioritization.
They make capacity math optional.
questions.explanation: Explicit assumptions create alignment and momentum.

questions.question: What is the strongest close-out move after the main design is presented?
options: Summarize key trade-offs, failure modes, and what changes under a new constraint.
Restate every component name in the diagram.
Switch to implementation details with no product relevance.
End immediately so no weaknesses are exposed.
questions.explanation: Strong wrap-ups demonstrate judgment and adaptability.
